mnemonics: Boundary is a Fence: A boundary point is like a fence post—no matter how small your yard (neighborhood) is, you'll find points from both inside the set and outside the set right next to it.
keyPoints: The boundary of a set A, denoted ∂A, is the set of points where every neighborhood intersects both A and its complement.
A point x is a boundary point if for every r > 0, the ball B(x, r) contains at least one point in A and one point not in A.
The boundary of a set is also equal to the closure of A minus the interior of A (∂A = cl(A) \ int(A)).
commonMistakes: Confusing boundary points with limit points; a boundary point is always a limit point, but the converse is not always true.
Assuming a set and its complement cannot both be closed; the boundary is a closed set and is the intersection of the closure of A and the closure of its complement.
